text:
Now I have a comprehensive understanding of the project. Let me plan the migration:

## Current Pages Router structure:
1. `pages/_app.js` - Root layout wrapper with AppProvider, header, nav, footer
2. `pages/_document.js` - HTML document structure with fonts, meta
3. `pages/404.js` - Custom 404 page
4. `pages/_error.js` - Custom error page (getInitialProps pattern)
5. `pages/index.js` - Homepage with getServerSideProps
6. `pages/blog/index.js` - Blog listing with getStaticProps + ISR
7. `pages/blog/[id].js` - Blog post detail with getStaticPaths + getStaticProps + ISR
8. `pages/api/posts/index.js` - API route for GET/POST posts
9. `pages/api/posts/[id].js` - API route for GET/PUT/DELETE post by id
10. `components/AppProvider.js` - Context provider
11. `styles/globals.css` - Global styles

## App Router migration plan:

### Key App Router patterns:
- `app/layout.tsx` replaces `_app.js` and `_document.js` - root layout with html/body tags
- `app/page.tsx` replaces `pages/index.js` - with server component pattern
- `app/not-found.tsx` replaces `pages/404.js`
- `app/error.tsx` + `app/global-error.tsx` replace `pages/_error.js` (getInitialProps -> error boundary pattern)
- `app/blog/page.tsx` replaces `pages/blog/index.js`
- `app/blog/[id]/page.tsx` replaces `pages/blog/[id].js`
- `app/api/posts/route.ts` replaces `pages/api/posts/index.js`
- `app/api/posts/[id]/route.ts` replaces `pages/api/posts/[id].js`

### Migration details:

1. **Root Layout (`app/layout.tsx`)**:
   - Combine `_app.js` and `_document.js` responsibilities
   - Use the `html` and `body` tags directly (no need for `_document.js`)
   - Include metadata, fonts preconnect
   - Wrap with AppProvider (client component since it uses useState)
   - Include header/nav and footer

2. **App Provider** needs to become a client component with "use client"

3. **Homepage (`app/page.tsx`)**:
   - Convert getServerSideProps to a server component that fetches directly
   - Use `headers()` from `next/headers` instead of `req.headers`
   - Use next/navigation instead of next/router
   - Type it properly with TypeScript

4. **Blog index (`app/blog/page.tsx`)**:
   - Convert getStaticProps to fetch data in the server component
   - Use `revalidate` export for ISR instead of `revalidate` in getStaticProps
   - Use `next/navigation` instead of `next/router`

5. **Blog post (`app/blog/[id]/page.tsx`)**:
   - Convert getStaticPaths to `generateStaticParams`
   - Convert getStaticProps to fetch in server component
   - Use `revalidate` export for ISR
   - Handle fallback with dynamic params (no loading state needed since fallback: 'blocking' -> just dynamic rendering)
   - Error handling with `notFound()` from `next/navigation`

6. **404 page (`app/not-found.tsx`)**:
   - Use `next/navigation` instead of `next/router`
   - Simpler layout

7. **Error page (`app/error.tsx`)**:
   - Use the error boundary pattern instead of getInitialProps
   - `app/error.tsx` catches client errors
   - `app/global-error.tsx` catches root layout errors

8. **API routes (`app/api/posts/route.ts` and `app/api/posts/[id]/route.ts`)**:
   - Use the Route Handlers pattern with `NextRequest`/`NextResponse`

9. **Convert all .js files to .tsx/.ts with proper types**

Let me create the task list and start implementing.

timeout a631fa2acb43034a7 local_agent running [Truncated. Full output: /tmp/claude-1001/-home-user-workspace/bc4e41cf-fdf3-4750-9481-b63121ce13a0/tasks/a631fa2acb43034a7.output] static shell consisting of HTML for initial page loads and a serialized [RSC Payload](/docs/app/getting-started/server-and-client-components#on-the-server) for client-side navigation, ensuring the browser receives fully rendered content instantly whether users navigate directly to the URL or transition from another page. 40 41 Next.js requires you to explicitly handle components that can't complete during prerendering. If they aren't wrapped in `` or marked with `use cache`, you'll see an [`Uncached data was accessed outside of `](https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/blocking-route) error during development and build time. 42 43 > **Good to know**: Caching can be applied at the component or function level, while fallback UI can be defined around any subtree, which means you can compose static, cached, and dynamic content within a single route. 44 45 52 53 This rendering approach is called **Partial Prerendering**, and it's the default behavior with Cache Components. For the rest of this document, we simply refer to it as "prerendering" which can produce a partial or complete output. 54 55 > **🎥 Watch:** Why Partial Prerendering and how it works → [YouTube (10 minutes)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MTcPrTIBkpA). 56 57 ## Automatically prerendered content 58 59 Operations like synchronous I/O, module imports, and pure computations can complete during prerendering. Components using only these operations have their rendered output included in the static HTML shell. 60 61 Because all operations in the `Page` component below complete during rendering, its rendered output is automatically included in the static shell. When both the layout and page prerender successfully, the entire route is the static shell. 62 63 ```tsx filename="page.tsx" 64 import fs from 'node:fs' 65 66 export default async function Page() { 67 // Synchronous file system read 68 const content = fs.readFileSync('./config.json', 'utf-8') 69 70 // Module imports 71 const constants = await import('./constants.json') 72 73 // Pure computations 74 const processed = JSON.parse(content).items.map((item) => item.value * 2) 75 76 return ( 77
78

{constants.appName}

79
    80 {processed.map((value, i) => ( 81
  • {value}
    • 82 ))} 83
84
85 ) 86 } 87 ``` 88 89 > **Good to know**: You can verify that a route was fully prerendered by checking the build output summary. Alternatively, see what content was added to the static shell of any page by viewing the page source in your browser. 90 91 ## Defer rendering to request time 92 93 During prerendering, when Next.js encounters work it can't complete (like network requests, accessing request data, or async operations), it requires you to explicitly handle it. To defer rendering to request time, a parent component must provide fallback UI using a Suspense boundary. The fallback becomes part of the static shell while the actual content resolves at request time. 94 95 Place Suspense boundaries as close as possible to the components that need them. This maximizes the amount of content in the static shell, since everything outside the boundary can still prerender normally. 96 97 > **Good to know**: With Suspense boundaries, multiple dynamic sections can render in parallel rather than blocking each other, reducing total load time. 98 99 ### Dynamic content 100 101 External systems provide content asynchronously, which often takes an unpredictable time to resolve and may even fail. This is why prerendering doesn't execute them automatically. 102 103 In general, when you need the latest data from the source on each request (like real-time feeds or personalized content), defer rendering by providing fallback UI with a Suspense boundary. 104 105 For example, the `DynamicContent` component below uses multiple operations that are not automatically prerendered. 106 107 ```tsx filename="page.tsx" 108 import { Suspense } from 'react' 109 import fs from 'node:fs/promises' 110 111 async function DynamicContent() { 112 // Network request 113 const data = await fetch('https://api.example.com/data') 114 115 // Database query 116 const users = await db.query('SELECT * FROM users') 117 118 // Async file system operation 119 const file = await fs.readFile('..', 'utf-8') 120 121 // Simulating external system delay 122 await new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, 100)) 123 124 return
Not in the static shell
125 } 126 ``` 127 128 To use `DynamicContent` within a page, wrap it in `` to define fallback UI: 129 130 ```tsx filename="page.tsx" 131 export default async function Page(props) { 132 return ( 133 <> 134

Part of the static shell

135 {/*

Loading..

is part of the static shell */} 136 Loading..

}> 137 138
Sibling excluded from static shell
139 140 141 ) 142 } 143 ``` 144 145 Prerendering stops at the `fetch` request. The request itself is not started, and any code after it is not executed. 146 147 The fallback (`

Loading...

`) is included in the static shell, while the component's content streams at request time. 148 149 In this example, since all operations (network request, database query, file read, and timeout) run sequentially within the same component, the content won't appear until they all complete. 150 151 > **Good to know**: For dynamic content that doesn't change frequently, you can use `use cache` to include the dynamic data in the static shell instead of streaming it. See the [during prerendering](#during-prerendering) section for an example. 152 153 ### Runtime data 154 155 A specific type of dynamic data that requires request context, only available when a user makes a request. 156 157 - [`cookies()`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cookies) - User's cookie data 158 - [`headers()`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/headers) - Request headers 159 - [`searchParams`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page#searchparams-optional) - URL query parameters 160 - [`params`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page#params-optional) - Dynamic route parameters (unless at least one sample is provided via [`generateStaticParams`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-static-params)). See [Dynamic Routes with Cache Components](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes#with-cache-components) for detailed patterns. 161 162 ```tsx filename="page.tsx" 163 import { cookies, headers } from 'next/headers' 164 import { Suspense } from 'react' 165 166 async function RuntimeData({ searchParams }) { 167 // Accessing request data 168 const cookieStore = await cookies() 169 const headerStore = await headers() 170 const search = await searchParams 171 172 return
Not in the static shell
173 } 174 ``` 175 176 To use the `RuntimeData` component, wrap it in a `` boundary: 177 178 ```tsx filename="page.tsx" 179 export default async function Page(props) { 180 return ( 181 <> 182

Part of the static shell

183 {/*

Loading..

is part of the static shell */} 184 Loading..

}> 185 186
Sibling excluded from static shell
187 188 189 ) 190 } 191 ``` 192 193 Use [`connection()`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/connection) if you need to defer to request time without accessing any of the runtime APIs above. 194 195 > **Good to know**: Runtime data cannot be cached with `use cache` because it requires request context. Components that access runtime APIs must always be wrapped in ``. However, you can extract values from runtime data and pass them as arguments to cached functions. See the [with runtime data](#with-runtime-data) section for an example. 196 197 One approach for reading runtime data like cookies without blocking the static shell is to pass a promise to a client context provider. See [Sharing data with context and React.cache](/docs/app/getting-started/server-and-client-components#sharing-data-with-context-and-reactcache) for an example. 198 199 > **Good to know:** `React.cache` operates in an isolated scope inside `use cache` boundaries. See [React.cache isolation](/docs/app/api-reference/directives/use-cache#reactcache-isolation) for more information. 200 201 ### Non-deterministic operations 202 203 Operations like `Math.random()`, `Date.now()`, or `crypto.randomUUID()` produce different values each time they execute. To ensure these run at request time (generating unique values per request), Cache Components requires you to explicitly signal this intent by calling these operations after dynamic or runtime data access. 204 205 ```tsx 206 import { connection } from 'next/server' 207 import { Suspense } from 'react' 208 209 async function UniqueContent() { 210 // Explicitly defer to request time 211 await connection() 212 213 // Non-deterministic operations 214 const random = Math.random() 215 const now = Date.now() 216 const date = new Date() 217 const uuid = crypto.randomUUID() 218 const bytes = crypto.getRandomValues(new Uint8Array(16)) 219 220 return ( 221
222

{random}

{now}

224

{date.getTime()}

225

{uuid}

226

{bytes}

227
228 ) 229 } 230 ``` 231 232 Because the `UniqueContent` component defers to request time, to use it within a route, it must be wrapped in ``: 233 234 ```tsx filename="page.tsx" 235 export default async function Page() { 236 return ( 237 //

Loading..

is part of the static shell 238 Loading..

}> 239 240 241 ) 242 } 243 ``` 244 245 Every incoming request would see different random numbers, date, etc. 246 247 > **Good to know**: You can cache non-deterministic operations with `use cache`. See the [with non-deterministic operations](#with-non-deterministic-operations) section for examples. 248 249 ## Using `use cache` 250 251 The [`use cache`](/docs/app/api-reference/directives/use-cache) directive caches the return value of async functions and components. You can apply it at the function, component, or file level. 252 253 Arguments and any closed-over values from parent scopes automatically become part of the [cache key](/docs/app/api-reference/directives/use-cache#cache-keys), which means different inputs produce separate cache entries. This enables personalized or parameterized cached content. 254 255 When [dynamic content](#dynamic-content) doesn't need to be fetched fresh from the source on every request, caching it lets you include the content in the static shell during prerendering, or reuse the result at runtime across multiple requests. 256 257 Cached content can be revalidated in two ways: automatically based on the cache lifetime, or on-demand using tags with [`revalidateTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidateTag) or [`updateTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/updateTag). 258 259 > **Good to know**: See [serialization requirements and constraints](/docs/app/api-reference/directives/use-cache#constraints) for details on what can be cached and how arguments work. 260 261 ### During prerendering 262 263 While [dynamic content](#dynamic-content) is fetched from external sources, it's often unlikely to change between accesses. Product catalog data updates with inventory changes, blog post content rarely changes after publishing, and analytics reports for past dates remain static. 264 265 If this data doesn't depend on [runtime data](#runtime-data), you can use the `use cache` directive to include it in the static HTML shell. Use [`cacheLife`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cacheLife) to define how long to use the cached data. 266 267 When revalidation occurs, the static shell is updated with fresh content. See [Tagging and revalidating](#tagging-and-revalidating) for details on on-demand revalidation. 268 269 ```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" highlight={1,4,5} 270 import { cacheLife } from 'next/cache' 271 272 export default async function Page() { 273 'use cache' 274 cacheLife('hours') 275 276 const users = await db.query('SELECT * FROM users') 277 278 return ( 279
    280 {users.map((user) => ( 281
  • {user.name}
    • 282 ))} 283
284 ) 285 } 286 ``` 287 288 The `cacheLife` function accepts a cache profile name (like `'hours'`, `'days'`, or `'weeks'`) or a custom configuration object to control cache behavior: 289 290 ```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" highlight={1,4-8} 291 import { cacheLife } from 'next/cache' 292 293 export default async function Page() { 294 'use cache' 295 cacheLife({ 296 stale: 3600, // 1 hour until considered stale 297 revalidate: 7200, // 2 hours until revalidated 298 expire: 86400, // 1 day until expired 299 }) 300 301 const users = await db.query('SELECT * FROM users') 302 303 return ( 304
    305 {users.map((user) => ( 306
  • {user.name}
    • 307 ))} 308
309 ) 310 } 311 ``` 312 313 > **Good to know:** A cache is considered "short-lived" when it uses the `seconds` profile, `revalidate: 0`, or `expire` under 5 minutes. Short-lived caches are automatically excluded from prerenders and become dynamic holes instead. If such a cache is nested inside another `use cache` without an explicit `cacheLife`, Next.js will throw an error during prerendering to prevent accidental misconfigurations. See [Prerendering behavior](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cacheLife#prerendering-behavior) for details. 314 315 See the [`cacheLife` API reference](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cacheLife) for available profiles and custom configuration options. 316 317 ### With runtime data 318 319 Runtime data and [`use cache`](/docs/app/api-reference/directives/use-cache) cannot be used in the same scope. However, you can extract values from runtime APIs and pass them as arguments to cached functions. 320 321 ```tsx filename="app/profile/page.tsx" 322 import { cookies } from 'next/headers' 323 import { Suspense } from 'react' 324 325 export default function Page() { 326 // Page itself creates the dynamic boundary 327 return ( 328 Loading...}> 329 330 331 ) 332 } 333 334 // Component (not cached) reads runtime data 335 async function ProfileContent() { 336 const session = (await cookies()).get('session')?.value 337 338 return 339 } 340 341 // Cached component/function receives data as props 342 async function CachedContent({ sessionId }: { sessionId: string }) { 343 'use cache' 344 // sessionId becomes part of cache key 345 const data = await fetchUserData(sessionId) 346 return
{data}
347 } 348 ``` 349 350 At request time, `CachedContent` executes if no matching cache entry is found, and stores the result for future requests. 351 352 ### With non-deterministic operations 353 354 Within a `use cache` scope, non-deterministic operations execute during prerendering. This is useful when you want the same rendered output served to all users: 355 356 ```tsx 357 export default async function Page() { 358 'use cache' 359 360 // Execute once, then cached for all requests 361 const random = Math.random() 362 const random2 = Math.random() 363 const now = Date.now() 364 const date = new Date() 365 const uuid = crypto.randomUUID() 366 const bytes = crypto.getRandomValues(new Uint8Array(16)) 367 368 return ( 369
370

371 {random} and {random2} 372

373

{now}

374

{date.getTime()}

375

{uuid}

376

{bytes}

377
378 ) 379 } 380 ``` 381 382 All requests will be served a route containing the same random numbers, timestamp, and UUID until the cache is revalidated. 383 384 ### Tagging and revalidating 385 386 Tag cached data with [`cacheTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cacheTag) and revalidate it after mutations using [`updateTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/updateTag) in Server Actions for immediate updates, or [`revalidateTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidateTag) when delays in updates are acceptable. 387 388 #### With `updateTag` 389 390 Use `updateTag` when you need to expire and immediately refresh cached data within the same request: 391 392 ```tsx filename="app/actions.ts" highlight={1,4,5,13} 393 import { cacheTag, updateTag } from 'next/cache' 394 395 export async function getCart() { 396 'use cache' 397 cacheTag('cart') 398 // fetch data 399 } 400 401 export async function updateCart(itemId: string) { 402 'use server' 403 // write data using the itemId 404 // update the user cart 405 updateTag('cart') 406 } 407 ``` 408 409 #### With `revalidateTag` 410 411 Use `revalidateTag` when you want to invalidate only properly tagged cached entries with stale-while-revalidate behavior. This is ideal for static content that can tolerate eventual consistency. 412 413 ```tsx filename="app/actions.ts" highlight={1,4,5,12} 414 import { cacheTag, revalidateTag } from 'next/cache' 415 416 export async function getPosts() { 417 'use cache' 418 cacheTag('posts') 419 // fetch data 420 } 421 422 export async function createPost(post: FormData) { 423 'use server' 424 // write data using the FormData 425 revalidateTag('posts', 'max') 426 } 427 ``` 428 429 For more detailed explanation and usage examples, see the [`use cache` API reference](/docs/app/api-reference/directives/use-cache). 430 431 ### What should I cache? 432 433 What you cache should be a function of what you want your UI loading states to be. If data doesn't depend on runtime data and you're okay with a cached value being served for multiple requests over a period of time, use `use cache` with `cacheLife` to describe that behavior. 434 435 For content management systems with update mechanisms, consider using tags with longer cache durations and rely on `revalidateTag` to mark static initial UI as ready for revalidation. This pattern allows you to serve fast, cached responses while still updating content when it actually changes, rather than expiring the cache preemptively. 436 437 ## Putting it all together 438 439 Here's a complete example showing static content, cached dynamic content, and streaming dynamic content working together on a single page: 440 441 ```tsx filename="app/blog/page.tsx" 442 import { Suspense } from 'react' 443 import { cookies } from 'next/headers' 444 import { cacheLife } from 'next/cache' 445 import Link from 'next/link' 446 447 export default function BlogPage() { 448 return ( 449 <> 450 {/* Static content - prerendered automatically */} 451
452

Our Blog

453
454 Home | About 455
456
457 458 {/* Cached dynamic content - included in the static shell */} 459 460 461 {/* Runtime dynamic content - streams at request time */} 462 Loading your preferences...

}> 463 464 465 466 ) 467 } 468 469 // Everyone sees the same blog posts (revalidated every hour) 470 async function BlogPosts() { 471 'use cache' 472 cacheLife('hours') 473 474 const res = await fetch('https://api.vercel.app/blog') 475 const posts = await res.json() 476 477 return ( 478
479

Latest Posts

480
    481 {posts.slice(0, 5).map((post: any) => ( 482
  • 483

    {post.title}

    484

    485 By {post.author} on {post.date} 486

    487
    • 488 ))} 489
490
491 ) 492 } 493 494 // Personalized per user based on their cookie 495 async function UserPreferences() { 496 const theme = (await cookies()).get('theme')?.value || 'light' 497 const favoriteCategory = (await cookies()).get('category')?.value 498 499 return ( 500
501

Your theme: {theme}

502 {favoriteCategory &&

Favorite category: {favoriteCategory}

} 503
504 ) 505 } 506 ``` 507 508 During prerendering the header (static) and the blog posts fetched from the API (cached with `use cache`), both become part of the static shell along with the fallback UI for user preferences. 509 510 When a user visits the page, they instantly see this prerendered shell with the header and blog posts. Only the personalized preferences need to stream in at request time since they depend on the user's cookies. This ensures fast initial page loads while still providing personalized content. 511 512 ## Metadata and Viewport 513 514 `generateMetadata` and `generateViewport` are part of rendering your page or layout. During prerendering, their access to runtime data or uncached dynamic data is tracked separately from the rest of the page. 515 516 If a page or layout is prerenderable but only metadata or viewport accesses uncached dynamic data or runtime data, Next.js requires an explicit choice: cache the data if possible, or signal that deferred rendering is intentional. See [Metadata with Cache Components](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-metadata#with-cache-components) and [Viewport with Cache Components](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-viewport#with-cache-components) for how to handle this. 517 518 ## Enabling Cache Components 519 520 You can enable Cache Components (which includes PPR) by adding the [`cacheComponents`](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/cacheComponents) option to your Next config file: 521 522 ```ts filename="next.config.ts" highlight={4} switcher 523 import type { NextConfig } from 'next' 524 525 const nextConfig: NextConfig = { 526 cacheComponents: true, 527 } 528 529 export default nextConfig 530 ``` 531 532 ```js filename="next.config.js" highlight={3} switcher 533 /** @type {import('next').NextConfig} */ 534 const nextConfig = { 535 cacheComponents: true, 536 } 537 538 module.exports = nextConfig 539 ``` 540 541 > **Good to know:** When Cache Components is enabled, `GET` Route Handlers follow the same prerendering model as pages. See [Route Handlers with Cache Components](/docs/app/getting-started/route-handlers#with-cache-components) for details. 542 543 ## Navigation uses Activity 544 545 When the [`cacheComponents`](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/cacheComponents) flag is enabled, Next.js uses React's [``](https://react.dev/reference/react/Activity) component to preserve component state during client-side navigation. 546 547 Rather than unmounting the previous route when you navigate away, Next.js sets the Activity mode to [`"hidden"`](https://react.dev/reference/react/Activity#activity). This means: 548 549 - Component state is preserved when navigating between routes 550 - When you navigate back, the previous route reappears with its state intact 551 - Effects are cleaned up when a route is hidden, and recreated when it becomes visible again 552 553 This behavior improves the navigation experience by maintaining UI state (form inputs, or expanded sections) when users navigate back and forth between routes. 554 555 > **Good to know**: Next.js uses heuristics to keep a few recently visited routes `"hidden"`, while older routes are removed from the DOM to prevent excessive growth. 556 557 ## Migrating route segment configs 558 559 When Cache Components is enabled, several route segment config options are no longer needed or supported: 560 561 ### `dynamic = "force-dynamic"` 562 563 **Not needed.** All pages are dynamic by default. 564 565 ```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" 566 // Before - No longer needed 567 export const dynamic = 'force-dynamic' 568 569 export default function Page() { 570 return
...
571 } 572 ``` 573 574 ```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" 575 // After - Just remove it 576 export default function Page() { 577 return
...
578 } 579 ``` 580 581 ### `dynamic = "force-static"` 582 583 Start by removing it. When unhandled dynamic or runtime data access is detected during development and build time, Next.js raises an error. Otherwise, the [prerendering](#automatically-prerendered-content) step automatically extracts the static HTML shell. 584 585 For dynamic data access, add [`use cache`](#using-use-cache) as close to the data access as possible with a long [`cacheLife`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cacheLife) like `'max'` to maintain cached behavior. If needed, add it at the top of the page or layout. 586 587 For runtime data access (`cookies()`, `headers()`, etc.), errors will direct you to [wrap it with `Suspense`](#runtime-data). Since you started by using `force-static`, you must remove the runtime data access to prevent any request time work. 588 589 ```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" 590 // Before 591 export const dynamic = 'force-static' 592 593 export default async function Page() { 594 const data = await fetch('https://api.example.com/data') 595 return
...
596 } 597 ``` 598 599 ```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" 600 import { cacheLife } from 'next/cache' 601 602 // After - Use 'use cache' instead 603 export default async function Page() { 604 'use cache' 605 cacheLife('max') 606 const data = await fetch('https://api.example.com/data') 607 return
...
608 } 609 ``` 610 611 ### `revalidate` 612 613 **Replace with `cacheLife`.** Use the `cacheLife` function to define cache duration instead of the route segment config. 614 615 ```tsx 616 // Before 617 export const revalidate = 3600 // 1 hour 618 619 export default async function Page() { 620 return
...
621 } 622 ``` 623 624 ```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" 625 // After - Use cacheLife 626 import { cacheLife } from 'next/cache' 627 628 export default async function Page() { 629 'use cache' 630 cacheLife('hours') 631 return
...
632 } 633 ``` 634 635 ### `fetchCache` 636 637 **Not needed.** With `use cache`, all data fetching within a cached scope is automatically cached, making `fetchCache` unnecessary. 638 639 ```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" 640 // Before 641 export const fetchCache = 'force-cache' 642 ``` 643 644 ```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" 645 // After - Use 'use cache' to control caching behavior 646 export default async function Page() { 647 'use cache' 648 // All fetches here are cached 649 return
...
650 } 651 ``` 652 653 ### `runtime = 'edge'` 654 655 **Not supported.** Cache Components requires Node.js runtime and will throw errors with [Edge Runtime](/docs/app/api-reference/edge). 656 "}]},"uuid":"b5633223-4a07-42ea-9f6b-71bc467a965a","timestamp":"2026-07-18T09:46:54.434Z","sourceToolAssistantUUID":"952fa121-5325-4e92-83f7-e369be8f647d","userType":"external","entrypoint":"sdk-cli","cwd":"/home/user/workspace","sessionId":"bc4e41cf-fdf3-4750-9481-b63121ce13a0","version":"2.1.207","gitBranch":"HEAD"} {"parentUuid":"b5633223-4a07-42ea-9f6b-71bc467a965a","isSidechain":true,"agentId":"a631fa2acb43034a7","message":{"id":"aeedc93b-eb48-46b5-8a8b-b996535d5ab8","type":"message","role":"assistant","model":"deepseek-v4-flash","content":[{"type":"thinking","thinking":"Now I have all the documentation read. Let me compile a comprehensive summary for each of the 7 topics the user asked about. Let me organize this by topic. Note that the docs structure has changed significantly from what the user expected - there's no `02-building-your-application` directory anymore. Instead, everything is under `01-app/01-getting-started/` and `01-app/02-guides/` and `01-app/03-api-reference/`. Key notes for Next.js 16 (this version): - Turbopack is the default bundler (use `--webpack` flag to opt into webpack) - `next build` no longer runs the linter automatically - Cache Components is an opt-in feature with `cacheComponents: true` in config - `use cache` directive is the new caching primitive - `cacheLife` and `cacheTag` replace many previous cache APIs - `revalidateTag` now supports `profile="max"` for stale-while-revalidate - `updateTag` is new for read-your-own-writes in Server Actions - `unstable_cache` is legacy/deprecated - `fetch` responses are NOT cached by default anymore - Route segment configs like `dynamic = "force-static"`, `revalidate`, `fetchCache` are being replaced by Cache Components patterns - `React.cache` has isolation issues within `use cache` boundaries - `params` and `searchParams` are Promises, must be awaited - PageProps, LayoutProps, RouteContext are globally available type helpers, no import needed - `next/link` now has `useLinkStatus` hook for loading states - `refresh()` from `next/cache` is new (replaces `router.refresh()`) - Metadata streaming is now supported - `connection()` API for deferring to request time - `server-only` and `client-only` are handled internally by Next.js - The pages-to-app migration doc references Next.js 13 and the old `pages/api` to `route.js` mapping - Dynamic rendering is now default with Cache Components; no need for `force-dynamic` Let me compile the full summary now.","signature":"aeedc93b-eb48-46b5-8a8b-b996535d5ab8"}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":14374,"cache_creation_input_tokens":0,"cache_read_input_tokens":60416,"output_tokens":0,"service_tier":"standard"}},"attributionAgent":"general-purpose","type":"assistant","uuid":"32eb7e11-b630-4f2d-9dc1-a91a91f4935f","timestamp":"2026-07-18T09:47:00.590Z","userType":"external","entrypoint":"sdk-cli","cwd":"/home/user/workspace","sessionId":"bc4e41cf-fdf3-4750-9481-b63121ce13a0","version":"2.1.207","gitBranch":"HEAD"}

text:
The agent is still running. Let me wait a bit more for it to complete, but I also have enough information from the partial output to understand the Next.js 16 patterns. Key findings:

1. Cache Components is opt-in (`cacheComponents: true`) - we'll skip this for the migration since it's a new opt-in feature
2. `params` and `searchParams` are Promises - must be awaited
3. `headers()` and `cookies()` are async - must be awaited
4. Route handlers use `NextRequest`/`NextResponse` pattern
5. `next/navigation` replaces `next/router`
6. Errors use `error.tsx` boundary pattern instead of `_error.js`
7. Not-found uses `not-found.tsx` instead of `404.js`
8. `generateStaticParams` replaces `getStaticPaths`
9. `layout.tsx` replaces `_app.js` and `_document.js`
10. ISR: In Next.js 16 (without Cache Components), we use `revalidate` export for ISR or `fetch(url, { next: { revalidate: N } })`

Let me wait for the agent and then start implementing. But actually, I have enough info to start. Let me begin the migration now.

For the migration, since `cacheComponents` is not enabled (it's opt-in), the standard App Router patterns apply:
- Server components by default
- `revalidate` segment config for ISR (NOT the `use cache` + `cacheLife` pattern, since that requires cacheComponents)
- `fetch` with `{ next: { revalidate: N } }` for per-fetch ISR
- `headers()` from `next/headers` is async
- Standard error boundary patterns

Actually wait - in Next.js 16, without Cache Components enabled, let me check what the data fetching patterns look like. The older patterns still work - `fetch` with `next: { revalidate }`, `revalidate` export on the page, etc. Let me read the specific data fetching docs.

Actually from what I saw in the output, the docs are heavily focused on Cache Components, and the standard patterns from 13/14/15 should still work. Let me just proceed with implementation.

Let me look at the patterns I need:

### Layout (app/layout.tsx)
- `metadata` export (not Head from next/head)
- `html` and `body` tags directly
- Font links in metadata or in the layout itself
- Wraps children in AppProvider (which needs 'use client')

### AppProvider (components/AppProvider.tsx)
- Add 'use client' directive
- Convert to TypeScript
- Use proper React types for children

### Homepage (app/page.tsx)
- Async server component
- Fetch data directly (no getServerSideProps needed)
- Use `headers()` for user agent
- Use `Link` from `next/link` for navigation
- Use `useRouter` from `next/navigation` (but in a client component)
- Wait - the current page has a `useRouter` call for `navigateToBlog`. Since this is a server component, we can't use hooks. We need to either:
  1. Keep it as a server component and use `<Link>` or `<button type="button" onClick={...}>` won't work in server components
  2. Make the button its own client component
  3. Just use `<Link>` instead of programmatic navigation

The simplest approach: convert the button to use `<Link>` or use a client component wrapper. Actually, `navigateToBlog` is just `router.push('/blog')` which can be done with `<Link href="/blog">`. Let's just replace the button with a Link component.

### Blog Index (app/blog/page.tsx)
- Async server component
- Fetch posts directly
- Export `revalidate = 60` for ISR

### Blog Post (app/blog/[id]/page.tsx)
- Async server component
- `generateStaticParams` for static generation
- Fetch post and comments
- Export `revalidate = 300` for ISR
- Handle not found with `notFound()` from `next/navigation`
- For the fallback loading state: since we're using `generateStaticParams` + `fallback: 'blocking'` equivalent, Next.js will just render dynamically for paths not in the list. We can use `loading.tsx` for the loading state.

### API Routes
- `app/api/posts/route.ts` - GET and POST handlers
- `app/api/posts/[id]/route.ts` - GET, PUT, DELETE handlers
- Use `NextRequest` and `NextResponse`

### Not Found (app/not-found.tsx)
- Use `useRouter` from `next/navigation`
- Or just use a Link

### Error (app/error.tsx + app/global-error.tsx)
- `'use client'` error boundary component
- Receives `error` and `reset` props
- TypeScript types

Let me start implementing now.

I have enough context from the partial docs. Key Next.js 16 patterns: `params` and `headers()` are async/Promises, `next/navigation` replaces `next/router`, and route handlers use `NextRequest`/`NextResponse`. Let me begin the migration.

- `next/router` → `next/navigation`
- Route handlers use `NextRequest`/`NextResponse`

- `fetch` is NOT cached by default in Next.js 16

text:
Build succeeded! Let me analyze the output:

1. `/` (homepage) - Dynamic (ƒ) - uses `cache: 'no-store'` and `headers()`, so it's server-rendered on demand
2. `/_not-found` - Static (○) - the built-in not-found page, stackable
3. `/api/posts` - Dynamic (ƒ) - API route handler
4. `/api/posts/[id]` - Dynamic (ƒ) - API route handler
5. `/blog` - Static (○) with 1m revalidate - ISR with 60 second revalidation
6. `/blog/[id]` - SSG (●) with 5m revalidate - uses `generateStaticParams` for 10 paths, ISR with 300 second revalidation

The blog posts show specific paths (blog/1 through blog/10) which matches the `generateStaticParams` slicing to 10 items.

Everything looks good! Let me update the task and also verify the project structure is clean.

One thing I want to double-check: The `app/api/posts/[id]/route.ts` - the original pages/api/posts/[id].js had 405 handling for unsupported methods. In App Router route handlers, unsupported methods automatically return 405, so that's handled.

Also, the original `_error.js` had `getInitialProps` to handle both 404 and 500 errors. In App Router, this is split into:
- `not-found.tsx` for 404 errors (triggered by `notFound()` call)
- `error.tsx` for 500-level errors (uncaught exceptions)
- `global-error.tsx` for errors in the root layout

This is actually better separation than the original.

Let me also check that the old `_document.js` functionality was properly migrated. The original had:
- `<Html lang="en">` → handled in `app/layout.tsx` with `<html lang="en">`
- `<Head>` with meta description and favicon → handled with `metadata` export
- Font preconnect and stylesheet links → handled in `<head>` in layout

text:
Migration complete. Here's a summary of what was done:

## Pages Router → App Router Migration

### Files Created (9 new `.tsx`/`.ts` files in `app/`)

| File | Purpose | Key Changes |
|---|---|---|
| `app/layout.tsx` | Root layout | Combines `_app.js` + `_document.js`. Uses `metadata` export instead of `<Head>`. Wraps with `AppProvider`, header/nav/footer |
| `app/page.tsx` | Homepage (`/`) | Async server component with `headers()` (awaited) for user agent, `fetch({ cache: 'no-store' })` for per-request data |
| `app/blog/page.tsx` | Blog listing (`/blog`) | Async server component with `export const revalidate = 60` for ISR |
| `app/blog/[id]/page.tsx` | Blog post (`/blog/[id]`) | `generateStaticParams` + async server component with `notFound()` error handling. `params` is awaited (Promise in Next.js 16) |
| `app/not-found.tsx` | 404 page | Replaces `pages/404.js`. Uses `<Link>` instead of `router.push()` |
| `app/error.tsx` | Error boundary | Replaces `pages/_error.js`. Uses `'use client'` error boundary with `error` + `reset` props instead of `getInitialProps` |
| `app/global-error.tsx` | Root error boundary | Catches errors in root layout. Has its own `<html>`/`<body>` |
| `app/api/posts/route.ts` | Posts API (GET/POST) | Route handler using `NextRequest`/`NextResponse` |
| `app/api/posts/[id]/route.ts` | Post API (GET/PUT/DELETE) | Route handler with typed `params: Promise<{ id: string }>` |

### Files Updated (1)
- `components/AppProvider.tsx` — Converted to TypeScript with `'use client'` directive, proper React types

### Files Removed (7)
- `pages/` directory entirely removed
- `components/AppProvider.js` — replaced by `.tsx` version

### Key App Router Patterns Used
- **`params` is a `Promise`** — always `await`ed
- **`headers()`/`cookies()` are async** — `await headers()` where needed
- **`next/navigation`** replaces `next/router` (`notFound()`, `Link`)
- **Metadata API** (`export const metadata`) replaces `next/head`
- **Route handlers** use Web `Request`/`Response` APIs with named HTTP method exports
- **Error boundaries** (`error.tsx` + `global-error.tsx`) replace `getInitialProps`
- **`generateStaticParams`** replaces `getStaticPaths`
- **ISR** via `fetch(url, { next: { revalidate } })`
